plugins.name: Git status
plugins.description: A git indicator in every session's header — a coloured dot and the branch of the repo that session works in, with the uncommitted and unpushed counts on hover, refreshing when the session runs a git command. Click it to drop the summary into that session. Plus a sortable dialog for the same across all your configured repositories; manage the repository list in settings.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: AC-36: a git-status settings option to hide the branch name in the session-header badge — only the coloured status dot stays on screen (branch name in the tooltip), freeing header width. Default on; updates live.
AC-33: the plugin's icons are drawn from the bundled Material Design set, so they render identically on every machine instead of relying on the OS emoji font.

The branch badge now follows a branch switch made outside the session — another terminal, or the IDE — not only git commands run inside it. It watches the repository's HEAD so a checkout or a deleted branch is reflected within a couple of seconds.
A Browse… button in settings opens a folder picker to add a repository instead of typing its path — it fills the box and adds it in one go. Needs nothing new from the host.

Contributes git steps to a workflow: switch to a branch (creating it), commit, push. It refuses to switch branches with uncommitted work, makes no empty commits, and never forces — force, reset and branch deletion are deliberately absent.

The git status moved out of the sidebar and into each session's own header: a coloured dot and the branch of the repo that session works in, counts on hover, click to drop the summary into that session. A sidebar section could only ever follow the selected session, which said nothing about the other panes on screen. Needs a cockpit build with the session-header contribution point.

Added an inline 'Session repo' panel that follows the active session — the git status of the repo it is working in, refreshing when you switch sessions or the session runs a git command. Needs a cockpit build with the session read/observe surface.
Initial release — per-repo branch / uncommitted / unpushed / ahead-behind via 'git status --porcelain --branch', with inject/copy of a summary.

plugins.name: Prompt Library
plugins.description: A library of reusable prompt templates in the left menu. Click one to insert it into the active session, filling in any {{variable}} fields first. Create, edit and remove templates in the same dialog. Plus a quick-insert palette (the Ctrl+Shift+P shortcut, or the command palette): search your prompts and drop one into the session with a click or Enter.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: AC-33: the plugin's icons are drawn from the bundled Material Design set, so they render identically on every machine instead of relying on the OS emoji font.
Deleting a template now asks for confirmation first. Needs a cockpit build with the confirmation dialog.
Quick-insert: subtle hover-grey selection (not the accent), and the search box now takes focus when opened from the shortcut.
Clearer selected-item highlight in the quick-insert list.
Fixed the quick-insert arrow keys not moving the selection.
Fixed the quick-insert search-bar icon rendering as a green emoji on Windows — now a monochrome accent chevron.
The quick-insert is now a spotlight-style search bar (rounded pill + accent icon) instead of a plain box.
Removed the redundant 'Insert prompt' menu button — the quick-insert stays on Ctrl+Shift+P and in the command palette.

Clearer selected-template highlight, plus a quick-insert palette (Insert prompt button / Ctrl+Shift+P) to search and drop a prompt into the session. Needs a cockpit build with the plugin shortcut surface.
Initial release - reusable prompt templates with {{variable}} substitution, click-to-insert into the active session, in-dialog management, five starter templates.

plugins.name: GitHub Issues
plugins.description: Browse open GitHub issues across your repos (via the gh CLI) or one repo in a searchable, sortable dialog, with an "Assigned to me" filter; click one to drop a prompt asking the agent to open and review it. An issue can also be started in Autopilot (the operator-triggered issue → merge-ready-PR pipeline), which posts its progress back as issue comments. The prompt template is editable in settings.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: Plan in Autopilot: send a GitHub issue to the Autopilot CEO to plan and run.
Issues can now be started in Autopilot (posts progress back as issue comments); requires host 0.3.0.
AC-33: the plugin's icons are drawn from the bundled Material Design set, so they render identically on every machine instead of relying on the OS emoji font.

Catches up with the plugin as it is built in the repo: the store had 1.7.0 while the manifest had moved on, so an installed copy never saw an update at all. Includes the workflow steps and triggers, and the "assigned to me" filter.

The same session badge and picker as YouTrack, with the actions GitHub actually has: assign to me, label, close as completed or not planned. An issue has no status field, so nothing pretends otherwise. Branch-name pattern and picker search terms are yours to set. Contributes a workflow trigger (issue picked) and steps (pick up, comment, close).
Added an "Assigned to me" filter (gh --assignee @me across repos, or the REST assignee filter for a single repo).
gh CLI + cross-repo, searchable/sortable dialog with a repository filter (All + per-repo), per-field settings help tooltips.
Initial release.

plugins.name: GitHub Pull Requests
plugins.description: Shows your open GitHub pull requests inline under the session list (how many is configurable, and you can limit it to specific repositories), refreshing both on a timer and the instant a session opens/merges/closes a PR (it watches session output for a pull url or a merged/closed line), via the gh CLI — the PRs you opened across all your repos, including org repos, or a single repo over HTTP — plus a dialog listing them in a searchable, sortable grid with an "Assigned to me" filter, and a Dashboard widget showing the same list as a resizable pane with its own item count; left-click a PR to drop a review prompt, or right-click for a menu (add to prompt / open in browser). Pull requests waiting for your review are listed separately under "Review requested", each with an Open button, and a new one raises a toast with an "Open in browser" button. The prompt template is editable in settings.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: AC-33: the plugin's icons are drawn from the bundled Material Design set, so they render identically on every machine instead of relying on the OS emoji font.

Adds a GitHub Pull Requests widget for the Dashboard workspace: the same open-PR list as the side-menu section — number, title, repository, an amber stripe on the ones waiting for your review, left-click to drop a review prompt — as a resizable pane with its own item count (1–20). Needs a cockpit build with the dashboard widget system for the widget to appear; the side-menu section works without it.

The list appears in seconds instead of the better part of a minute. The archived-repository list was asked of gh once per owner — three seconds a call, fourteen calls — for an answer already present in the repository list that had just been fetched; those calls are gone. The owner searches now run at once instead of one after another. And the last list is shown the moment the section appears, while the fresh one loads behind it: an empty panel does not read as "loading", it reads as "no open pull requests".

Watch every repository you are involved with — owned, collaborated on, or reached through an organisation — with no list to maintain: gh works out which repositories those are. The list is ordered by last activity, newest first, and the dialog has an Updated column. Right-click a row to set aside a pull request, or every pull request from a repository that is never your business. A loading bar while gh is fetching, and searching no longer brings back what you ignored.

Watch a repository: every open pull request in an owner or an owner/repo, whoever opened it. The searches so far all asked "which of these are mine" — authored by you, assigned to you, waiting on your review — which is the right question for a personal list and the wrong one for a project you are responsible for: five open pull requests in a repo of yours, none of them yours, showed nothing at all. Also: pull requests in archived repositories are excluded everywhere (the exclusion used to apply only when the view was scoped to a single owner, so your own list and your review requests still showed them — and a pull request in an archived repo cannot be merged, reviewed or closed). Plus the workflow templates the previous build shipped.

Contributes an "Open a pull request" step (draft by default) and a "Pull request merged" trigger that fires when one of yours goes in — asked of GitHub every few minutes, and silent on the first look, or every merge you ever made would fire at once.
The inline PR count is now configurable (1-20), and you can limit the list to specific repositories (owner/repo per line; blank = all).
Added a right-click context menu to the inline PR rows: 'Add to prompt' (the normal left-click action) and 'Open in browser'.

Smart refresh — the inline section now also refreshes the instant a session opens, merges or closes a PR, by watching session output for a pull url or a merged/closed line (on top of the 60s timer). Needs a cockpit build with the session read/observe surface.
Added: the inline PR section auto-refreshes (60s) so PRs appear/disappear on their own as they are opened, merged or closed.
Fixed: showed no PRs for the default @me owner — now searches --author @me (your PRs across all repos, including org repos) instead of --owner @me (only repos you own).
Added an "Assigned to me" filter (gh --assignee @me across repos, or a client-side assignees filter for a single repo).
Inline Open PRs section + all-PRs dialog, gh CLI or single-repo HTTP. Hardened JSON parsing; per-field settings help tooltips; settings apply live (no restart).

plugins.name: YouTrack
plugins.description: A left-menu button (or the Shift+Y shortcut) opens a dialog listing open issues across one or more configured YouTrack instances (over HTTP with a permanent token per instance — YouTrack has no CLI), with instance/project/state filters and an "Assigned to me" filter, in a searchable, sortable grid; click one to drop a prompt asking the agent to work on it. Run the ticket workflow from the cockpit: Start an issue (move it to in progress, assign it to you, and tie it to the session you work in), or move it to any state the board itself allows — including workflow-governed boards, whose allowed transitions are read rather than assumed. A linked issue appears with its status in that session's own header, with quick actions (move state, copy branch name, open in browser). An issue can also be started in Autopilot (the operator-triggered issue → merge-ready-PR pipeline), which posts its progress and moves the issue's stage back through this plugin. The prompt template is editable in settings. Also registers each instance's JetBrains remote MCP server so sessions can query YouTrack directly as tools. Screenshots you send with a message are attached automatically to the issue the agent creates or updates that turn (a global setting, on by default).
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: Plan in Autopilot: send a YouTrack issue to the Autopilot CEO to plan and run.
Issues can now be started in Autopilot (posts progress and moves the issue stage); requires host 0.3.0.

AC-116: images you send with a message are attached automatically to the issue the agent creates or updates that turn — a global setting in the plugin settings, on by default. Replaces AC-14's per-session toggle; adds a fallback attach_message_images_to_issue tool.
AC-14: attach images you send with a message to the issue the session tracks — opt-in per session in the header menu.
AC-33: the plugin's icons are drawn from the bundled Material Design set, so they render identically on every machine instead of relying on the OS emoji font.

Plugin-owned MCP (AC-11): the instance's YouTrack MCP server is the plugin's own — it no longer shows in the MCP-servers manager but is offered per-session (uncheckable) in the New-session picker. A per-instance toggle chooses whether an instance contributes its MCP server, and a migration reclaims the entries an earlier version pushed into the registry.

Catches up with the plugin as it is built in the repo (the store stood at 1.10.0, so an installed copy never saw an update): the ticket-status-changed trigger with the statuses read from your board, the workflow templates, and the settings reachable from the dialog itself.

The session header picks the ticket: one menu action per issue tracker instead of a button each, and the badge appears only when a session is actually tracking something. Status moves follow the board's own order — forward, back, or anywhere it allows. The branch-name pattern and which issues the picker shows are yours to set. Contributes a workflow trigger (ticket picked) and a step (set status).
Added a Shift+Y keyboard shortcut that opens the issues dialog (via the new plugin shortcut SDK). Needs a cockpit build with the keyboard-shortcuts system.
Added an "Assigned to me" filter (YouTrack's "for: me" clause added to the query).

Registers the JetBrains remote YouTrack MCP server (/mcp, per configured instance) so a session can query YouTrack via tools. Requires YouTrack Cloud 2026.2+ with the MCP server enabled + a permanent token.
Multiple YouTrack instances; left-menu button opening a dialog with instance/project/state filters (All). Per-field settings help tooltips; no personal instance baked in.
Single-instance per-project open issues over the YouTrack REST API.

plugins.name: Gemini / OpenAI Provider
plugins.description: Adds Gemini and OpenAI as selectable session providers, both over an OpenAI-compatible chat-completions endpoint (Gemini's own OpenAI-compat endpoint, or api.openai.com directly) via Microsoft.Extensions.AI. Configure an API key and model per profile in Manage profiles.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: Applies the host's hidden per-session system prompt (embedded run brief).

Experimental first provider-plugin (#45 phase A): Gemini + OpenAI via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint over Microsoft.Extensions.AI. Trimmed capability flags; API key redacted from ToString; graceful handling when the provider plugin is missing.

plugins.name: CLI Agent Provider (Codex)
plugins.description: Adds the Codex CLI as a provider, in two ways. As a TTY session it runs the real interactive Codex TUI in a pane, the same as Claude's — its own interface, its own approval prompts, which the cockpit cannot see into. As an SDK session it is driven headless, a subprocess per turn (`codex exec --json`); that route has no in-band permission channel at all, because Codex sets approval-policy to never in headless mode. Configure the sandbox per profile. Requires the codex CLI installed and authenticated (`codex login`) on the machine running Cockpit.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: Declares file-access confinement to the working directory (Codex workspace-write), so an isolated Autopilot step on Codex is allowed to run confined.

AC-77 fix: Codex rejected every shared MCP server whose name has a space or colon ("YouTrack: Personal", "SQL Explorer") with "Invalid MCP server name: must match pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$", so 0.4.0 injected the servers but they failed at startup. The server name is now folded to Codex's charset (out-of-set characters become _), so the servers actually start; the Cockpit display name and the Claude route are unchanged.

AC-77: the interactive Codex TUI now receives the session's Cockpit MCP servers as `-c mcp_servers.*` config overrides — previously only the headless app-server route got them, so the TUI saw only its own ~/.codex servers. A bearer token rides the process environment, and a cockpit-hosted server references COCKPIT_MCP_KEY, so no secret lands on the command line.

AC-20: managed provider CLI. The cockpit can install and keep the codex binary up to date in its own location (download → digest verify → extract the release tarball), preferred over PATH yet override-able and removable, with an Install/Update/Remove panel in the profile settings.

AC-40: for a cockpit-hosted MCP the Codex config now points at the COCKPIT_MCP_KEY environment variable (bearer_token_env_var) instead of a literal token, so this run's endpoint key never lands in a config file on disk.

Per-profile environment variables (AC-22): declares SupportsEnvVars and applies the profile's variables to the app-server spawn, laid under the config's own CODEX_HOME/auth so those keep the last word. Also carries the previously unpublished 0.1.2 executable-resolution fallback (~/.local/bin, ~/.bun/bin) for AppImage/GUI launches.
Phase B1: Codex CLI subprocess provider through the plugin session-driver contract (process-per-turn, resume, stderr-drain, interrupt=kill). Live Codex schema/auth/resume verification pending (B2).

plugins.name: GitHub Models
plugins.description: Adds GitHub Models as a selectable session provider, over its OpenAI-compatible chat-completions endpoint (models.github.ai/inference) via Microsoft.Extensions.AI. Configure a GitHub personal access token (models:read scope) and model per profile in Manage profiles. Experimental.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: Applies the host's hidden per-session system prompt (embedded run brief).
GitHub Models via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint over Microsoft.Extensions.AI (same driver as the Gemini/OpenAI provider). Needs a GitHub PAT with models:read.

plugins.name: Claude Transcript Search
plugins.description: Search everything you and the agent ever wrote in a Claude CLI session. Opens on the conversations you worked on last so resuming one is a click, searches back through the rest by any text you or the agent wrote, and hands a conversation straight to the New-session dialog.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: AC-33: the plugin's icons are drawn from the bundled Material Design set, so they render identically on every machine instead of relying on the OS emoji font.

Resuming a conversation from the search picker now starts the session in the folder it ran in, so it finds that conversation's history instead of opening a fresh one — the picker hands the New-session dialog the working directory alongside the id.

The dialog now opens on the ten conversations you worked on last, each summarised by the prompt you started it with — resuming one is usually a matter of the last few sessions rather than of remembering a word to search for. Clearing the search box returns to that list.

Transcript search moved out of the cockpit's core into this plugin — the JSONL history is Claude's own format, and a multi-provider core has no business parsing it. Keeps the Ctrl+F shortcut and the sidebar button, and adds a picker behind the New-session dialog's Search button so resuming a conversation no longer means typing a session id by hand. Needs a cockpit build with the conversation-picker contribution point.

plugins.name: Workflows
plugins.description: A visual editor for cockpit workflows, and an engine that runs them: drop triggers, actions and decisions on a canvas, wire them together, configure each step, and press Run. Every run is kept — which step got what, what it produced, how long it took — because "it did not work" is not something you can act on. A step this build cannot execute is passed by with a reason, never counted as a success.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: Fixes the zoom-in button so the whole zoom toolbar is vector icons (it was still a text '+'), and aligns the plugin's internal version with its manifest.
AC-33: the plugin's icons are drawn from the bundled Material Design set, so they render identically on every machine instead of relying on the OS emoji font.

Security fix (AC-62): the run_workflow MCP tool now refuses a disarmed (inactive) flow, so the operator's arm switch gates the agent route too — an agent can no longer run a flow the operator never armed or deliberately disabled. Running a disarmed flow by hand from the editor is unaffected. Complements the AC-38 consent gate.

AC-40: the cockpit-workflows MCP is the cockpit's own loopback endpoint, so it is no longer listed in the MCP-servers manager; a new toggle in the plugin's own settings view turns it on or off for sessions.

Consent gate (AC-38): a step that acts with your rights — a command, a session hand-off, or egress (http, Slack/Discord) — is put to you for Approve/Deny before it runs, showing the literal action (the command, the URL and body, the full webhook URL). A run you start yourself is never asked; a trigger asks unless you mark the flow Run unattended. An MCP agent cannot create or arm a flow that contains a dangerous step. Plugin-contributed steps declare their own consent in code. Needs a cockpit build from after the AC-38 merge (plugin SDK / Abstractions 1.5.0).

Security fix (AC-39): a value substituted from an earlier step into a command step is shell-quoted, so untrusted step data can no longer break out of its argument and run as a command. Operator templates keep their own shell features.

A workflow MCP (cockpit-workflows) so agents can list, read, run and create/edit workflows through their own tools (AC-12), and a 'Set session status' step that labels the session a flow started with the ticket it picked up (AC-13). Needs a cockpit build with the plugin-MCP endpoint support.
Catches up with the plugin as it is built in the repo (the store stood at 0.18.0): the Switch step, an error exit per step, and templates you can export and import.

First release: a canvas where flows are drawn and an engine that runs them. Triggers that fire by themselves (text in a session, a schedule), steps that use what the steps before produced ({output}, {Step.field}), decisions on real expressions ({= …}), a three-pane node dialog showing what comes in and what came out, run history, and Slack/Discord steps. Other plugins contribute their own steps and triggers.

First release: a canvas where flows are drawn and an engine that runs them. Triggers that fire by themselves (text in a session, a schedule), steps that use what the steps before produced ({output}, {Step.field}), decisions on real expressions ({= …}), a three-pane node dialog showing what comes in and what came out, run history, and Slack/Discord steps. Other plugins contribute their own steps and triggers.

plugins.name: Clock
plugins.description: The time and date, for a Dashboard workspace. Ships with the cockpit, so a new dashboard has something on it from the start — it is here so you can update it on its own, or put it back if you removed it.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: AC-33: the plugin's icons are drawn from the bundled Material Design set, so they render identically on every machine instead of relying on the OS emoji font.
Initial release — the time and date on a dashboard. Needs a cockpit build with the widget contribution point (AddWidget).

plugins.name: System Monitor
plugins.description: CPU, memory and disk usage for a Dashboard workspace. You pick which of the three in its settings, so two of them side by side can each watch something different.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: AC-33: the plugin's icons are drawn from the bundled Material Design set, so they render identically on every machine instead of relying on the OS emoji font.
Initial release — CPU, memory and disk on a dashboard, each of the three toggleable per placed instance. Needs a cockpit build with the widget contribution point (AddWidget).

plugins.name: Claude (bundled)
plugins.description: Claude as a provider plugin (Fase 4). Runs the real interactive Claude TUI in a pane (TTY) and, headless, drives it over stream-json (SDK) with the cockpit's permission prompts, live model/permission/effort switching, and usage limits. Bundled and pre-approved: out of the box nothing changes. Requires the claude CLI installed and logged in on the machine running Cockpit.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: Declares file-access confinement to the working directory, so an isolated Autopilot step on Claude is allowed to run confined.
0.4.2 — fix: interleaving TTY and SDK sessions no longer lose their MCP servers (atomic ~/.claude.json trust write)
Fix: pasting an image into a Claude SDK chat session is now attached and sent to the model (the provider registration now declares image/vision support).

AC-20: managed provider CLI. The cockpit can install and keep the claude binary up to date in its own location (download → SHA-256 verify against the release manifest → place), preferred over PATH yet override-able (pin) and removable, with an Install/Update/Remove panel plus an update check in the profile settings.

AC-40: for a cockpit-hosted MCP the Claude mcp-config now references the COCKPIT_MCP_KEY environment variable (Bearer ${COCKPIT_MCP_KEY}) instead of embedding a literal token, so this run's endpoint key never lands in a config file on disk.

Sets COCKPIT_PANE_ID in the SDK spawn's environment (AC-13), so an SDK-session agent can name its own session to the cockpit-session MCP server's set_status tool — the same identity the TTY route already gets. Needs a cockpit build with the cockpit-session MCP.

First store release of the bundled Claude provider. Per-profile environment variables (AC-22): declares SupportsEnvVars and applies the profile's variables to the SDK spawn before the driver's own rules, so ANTHROPIC_* stays dropped and CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR keeps the last word.

plugins.name: GitHub Actions
plugins.description: The GitHub Actions status of the branch a session is working in, shown in that session's header: a coloured dot (green pass, red fail, amber running) for the latest workflow run on the current branch, with the workflow, event and time on hover — click to open the run on GitHub. Completes the GitHub set alongside Issues, Pull Requests and Git status. Uses your existing gh CLI login (no token to paste); requires the GitHub CLI installed and authenticated.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: AC-52: initial release — the GitHub Actions status of the branch a session is working in, shown in that session's header (green pass, red fail, amber running), click to open the run on GitHub.

plugins.name: Session Review
plugins.description: A "Review changes" action in each session's header opens a panel showing what that session changed — the git diff of its working directory, coloured for reading — with one click to ask the session to review its own changes. Makes the cockpit a review station, not just a runner: the quality guard before an agent's output lands under your name. Requires git installed on the machine running Cockpit.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: AC-50: initial release — a Review changes action in each session's header shows the uncommitted git diff of that session's working directory (coloured), with one click to ask the session to review its own changes.

plugins.name: Kubernetes
plugins.description: Register Kubernetes clusters and give agents scoped, human-approved access to them through an mcp__cockpit-k8s__* server. The plugin talks to the cluster itself (via the official Kubernetes client) and keeps the credentials — an agent never gets a kubeconfig. Every call is gated: opening a cluster asks for consent, a namespace outside the cluster's allowed list asks each session (reads included), and every change asks afresh and is never remembered. Cluster-scoped resources, exec and port-forward are off until you turn them on per cluster. A port-forward tunnel shows in the status bar with an operator-only Kill and auto-closes. Configure clusters in the plugin settings: point at a kubeconfig file (e.g. ~/.kube/config, read live) and pick a context from it, or paste a kubeconfig which is stored under the secret layer.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: Security fix (AC-92): the exec-consent prompt now escapes newlines and tabs visibly, so a multi-line command cannot masquerade as a single, commented-out line while the raw multi-line command runs against the cluster.
Adds a Sessions-toolbar quick action to open Kubernetes settings (AC-91).

Initial release, hardened after a full code & security review: cluster-scoped consent is remembered per kind (not per cluster), API/exec-auth errors never leak host detail to the agent, a live port-forward survives a settings save, duplicate cluster labels fail closed, and list results flag truncation.

plugins.name: Docker
plugins.description: Register the local Docker daemon and give agents scoped, human-approved access to it through an mcp__cockpit-docker__* server. The plugin talks to the Docker Engine API itself (via Docker.DotNet) and keeps the connection — an agent never gets the Docker socket. Covers day-to-day dev operations: read the daemon and containers (list, inspect, logs, stats, top, images, volumes, networks, compose ps/config/logs), change state (start/stop/restart/remove, pull, tag, push, compose up/down/build/restart), reclaim disk (prune, remove volume), and build or copy files (build_image, cp) — the last two behind an off-by-default exec capability. Every call is gated: the first touch of the daemon asks for consent (reads are free after that); every change asks afresh with the literal command shown and is never remembered; destructive and exec actions show their target verbatim. Detached containers this plugin starts show in the status bar with an operator-only Kill. Configure the daemon endpoint in the plugin settings (blank uses the local default socket).
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: Rounds out day-to-day dev operations (AC-93): adds logs, compose_logs, list_images, pull_image, inspect, stats, top, list_volumes, list_networks, tag, push, prune, remove_volume, build_image, cp, compose_ps and compose_restart — each with a deliberate consent level (reads free after the one-time daemon consent; changes ask afresh; destructive and exec actions show the target/command). A run against a missing local image now gives a clear 'pull it first' error instead of a misleading daemon-unreachable one.

Initial release: ps/inspect/logs, start/stop/restart/remove, exec and run (behind an off-by-default capability), and Docker Compose up/down/build/config via the CLI — each gated by a human consent prompt that shows the literal command. Sibling of the Kubernetes plugin; built and hardened alongside a full code & security review. Contributes a Sessions-toolbar quick action to open Docker settings (AC-91).

plugins.name: Autopilot
plugins.description: Operator-triggered "issue → merge-ready PR" pipeline. Start a YouTrack or GitHub issue into a visible, self-driving agent session in its own isolated worktree; it works the item behind a quality gate (visual verify, code review, security review, conventions), posts progress and questions back to the issue and moves its stage as it goes, and stops at a merge-ready PR — you approve the run before it starts and always do the merge yourself.
plugins.author: Cockpit

versions.notes: Name the folder an Autopilot run works in — pick a recent/pinned folder or browse, and the CEO can propose it. A git repository isolates each step in a worktree; a plain folder (an admin task with no repo) now runs in it directly, confined to that folder, instead of failing at the first step.

Autopilot CEO: plan a run, approve once, autonomous to merge-ready — each step in the run's own worktree on the model you pick (incl. confined local models), code+security review gates, queue and history, Plan in Autopilot from YouTrack/GitHub.
First full release of Autopilot: the operator-triggered issue → merge-ready-PR pipeline (needs host 0.3.0).

templates.name: Morning briefing
templates.description: Every morning: what happened in the repo since yesterday, handed to the agent with "what should I pick up first?".
templates.author: Cockpit

templates.name: Nightly tests → only tell me if they fail
templates.description: Runs the tests at night and says nothing when they pass. A notification you get every night is one you stop reading.
templates.author: Cockpit

templates.name: Build fails → tell the team
templates.description: Runs your build; a failure leaves by the red error wire and lands on Discord with what actually went wrong. The shortest way to see what an error path is for.
templates.author: Cockpit

templates.name: Ask me first, then deploy
templates.description: A deploy that stops and asks you before it runs, and says on Discord whether it worked. For the steps that are not free to undo.
templates.author: Cockpit

templates.name: Health check → tell me when it is down
templates.description: Asks a URL every hour. A bad status tells you; no answer at all tells you differently — that is the error path, not the same thing.
templates.author: Cockpit

templates.name: Session says it is done → tell me
templates.description: Watches your sessions for a line you name ("All tests passed") and tells you on Discord. Wait for an agent without watching one.
templates.author: Cockpit

templates.name: Delegate a review to a local model
templates.description: Takes your staged diff, hands it to a local model as a background task, and shows you what it said. The review you were not going to do yourself.
templates.author: Cockpit

templates.name: Ticket picked → open a session on it
templates.description: Picking a ticket opens a session in the repo it belongs to, already told what the ticket is.
templates.author: Cockpit

templates.name: Ticket reaches Review → tell the team
templates.description: When you move a ticket, a Switch sends Review and Done down their own wires, each with its own message on Discord.
templates.author: Cockpit

templates.name: Agent says it is done → comment on the issue
templates.description: When a session says the work is finished, the issue it belongs to hears about it — where the next person looks.
templates.author: Cockpit
